article.plain_text:
The person who built Claude Code at Anthropic stopped prompting Claude.
 
His name is Boris Cherny. In June 2026 he said it out loud: "I don't prompt Claude anymore."
Loops prompt Claude for him. His actual job now is writing loops.
That sounds like a flex. But no. It's the biggest shift in how people use Claude and ChatGPT right now. You've probably heard the phrase. Almost nobody does it yet.
Here is how you work right now.
You type a prompt. 
Claude edits a file. 
You run the test. 
It breaks. 
You paste the error back. 
It tries again. 
Twenty minutes later you realize you've been babysitting the exact thing you wanted to hand off.
You are the loop. You're the one checking the work and deciding the next step, every single time. That is the job a loop takes over.
One example shows the whole difference
Ask Claude to write you a one page brief on any topic. Simple task. It writes something clean and confident, with sources at the bottom.
 
Now read the sources. Some of them are fake(!!!). Claude made them up and has no idea it did. They look real. The links go nowhere, or they go somewhere that doesn't say what Claude claimed. This is the quiet way Claude burns you, and a single prompt can never catch it, because Claude stays confident it's right until something opens the link.
Now run it as a loop instead. Same brief, but you add a bar it can measure: 
every claim needs at least three sources, and every link has to open to a real page that backs up the claim.
Watch what happens. Claude writes the brief, then goes link by link. It opens each one. It throws out the dead ones and the fake ones. It finds real replacements. It keeps checking until every single source on the page is something you can open. Then it stops.
It never gets bored. It never skips the boring ones.
Here's how you'd write that in Claude Code. Don't worry about the command yet, I break it down below:
 
What a loop is
 
A loop is a small system that prompts Claude for you, over and over, until a job is done.
Every loop, no matter how fancy, is the same five beats:
Find the work. Open tasks, failing tests, unread emails, files in a folder.
Do it. Claude handles one item at a time, like you would by hand.
Check itself. A second pass confirms the work is done and correct, not just produced.
Remember. It writes down what's finished, so it never repeats work or loses its place.
Go again. It repeats until nothing's left, then stops or pings you.
One line to keep: prompting is doing the work. Loop engineering is managing the worker.
"Isn't this just a scheduled task?"
 
Good question. No.
You can already make your computer run the same thing every morning at 8am. That's a cron job. It's older than most of us. It runs a fixed script. Same steps, every time, no thinking.
A loop is different because of one thing: there's a decision-maker inside it.
A cron job runs a script. A loop runs Claude. Claude looks at the current situation, picks the next action, does it, checks the result, and then decides what to do next. Keep going. Try again. Undo. Stop.
That decision in the middle is the entire point. A script can't look at a broken test and figure out a different fix. Claude can. This only became possible once Claude and ChatGPT got good enough to make real judgment calls mid-job.
The two commands that run a loop
 
Here's where most people go wrong. You don't build a loop by typing "do this in a loop" into a normal chat. Claude Code has two commands built in, and which one you reach for depends on the kind of loop you need.
1. /goal is the loop that works until it's done.
 
But obviously not that kind of goal. xD
You saw /goal up in the brief example. You type it, then describe what "done" looks like. Claude keeps working, turn after turn, on its own. After every turn, a second copy of Claude quietly checks: are we at the goal yet? If not, it tells the first one why, and the work continues. The moment the goal is met, the loop stops by itself.
That self-check after every turn is the whole difference between a real loop and a prompt that runs once and hopes.
Use /goal when there's a finish line. Work until this is true.
2. /loop is the loop that repeats on a rhythm.
Reach for this when the job isn't "finish a pile" but "keep an eye on something." You tell it how often and what to do, and Claude re-runs it for you.
 
The 30m means every 30 minutes. You can also just say "every morning, triage my inbox" and Claude will schedule it.
Use /loop when there's no finish line, just a beat. Check this again and again.
Most of the strong loops you'll build start with /goal. These are recent Claude Code features, so if you don't see the commands, update Claude Code and they'll show up.
Your first loop, ready to paste
A one line goal is plenty for a small job. For a bigger job with lots of steps, you hand Claude a full charter: where to find the work, how to check itself, how to remember, when to stop.
Fill in the brackets and paste this into Claude Code:
You are running as a loop, not answering one prompt. Here is your charter.
 
The state file is the quiet hero. Without it, every run starts from zero. With it, the loop picks up exactly where it left off, even when it runs on a schedule.
When not to build a loop
Loops are not free and not for everything. Three honest things before you start.
One-off tasks don't need a loop. If the job is a single answer, a plain prompt is faster. Loops earn their setup cost on work that repeats or has many pieces.
Loops cost more. A loop that checks itself and retries is running Claude several times per item. On a Claude plan, you'll hit your usage limit faster. 
Vague work doesn't belong in a loop. "Think of a better product strategy" is not a loop. Go figure out the actual goal first.
Start this week
Pick one task you keep doing by hand, the kind with lots of small pieces. Paste the charter. Fill in the brackets. Run it once while you watch every step.
When you trust it, put it on a schedule. The first time you wake up to work that finished overnight, you'll stop typing prompts one at a time. Just like Boris.
 
Helped? Follow me. I share this stuff so you don't have to dig for it

articles.feedfree_summary_3_lines:
Makes the case that the user is often the real loop, manually checking output and feeding back errors Claude should learn to catch itself.
Uses fake citations in research tasks to show why one-shot prompting fails and why measurable verification loops matter.
Recasts loops as a way to offload the checking-and-deciding cycle, not just the initial generation step.

article.plain_text:
I set up one file called CLAUDE.md and it quietly fixed most of the things that used to drive me crazy about Claude.
A single CLAUDE.md file recently hit the top of GitHub Trending with 82,000 stars and 7,800 forks. Most people who use Claude have never set one up, and the few who have aren't sure what to put in it. That gap is costing them hours every week.
Here's the problem it solves. Every time you open a new Claude session, it starts with a blank memory. It doesn't know your name, your work, your preferences or how you like things done. So you burn the first few minutes re-explaining everything, or you skip it and get back something that doesn't fit how you work. CLAUDE.md fixes that once and for good.
 
This Is Not Just a Developer Tool
CLAUDE.md is an instruction file that Claude reads on its own at the start of every session, and it helps anyone who uses Claude regularly.
Writers use it to lock in their voice so Claude never sounds like someone else. Marketers use it to define their audience so the copy stops coming out generic. Researchers use it to set how they want information structured. Business owners use it to hand Claude the full context of their company so every output fits their reality.
 
Without it, you start from zero every session. You repeat yourself, you correct the same mistakes, you explain your preferences for the hundredth time. CLAUDE.md is the first thing worth setting up before any serious work with Claude, whatever you use it for.
 
How to Create the File (2 minutes)
1. Open your project folder and create a new file. Name it exactly "CLAUDE.md", capital letters, no spaces. It's just a plain text file with a .md extension.
2. Open it in any text editor, Notepad, TextEdit, VS Code, whatever you use. You paste your instructions straight into it as plain text.
3. Copy the instructions from this article that fit you and paste them in. You don't need all 21, start with 3 or 4 that solve your biggest frustrations.
4. Save the file. Claude Code reads it automatically every time you open a session in that folder. No setup, no extra steps, it works from the first message.
 
 
How Claude Talks to You
1. Kill the filler
By default Claude opens with "Great question!", "Of course!", "Certainly!", "Absolutely!", phrases that add nothing and eat your time. When you're in Claude for hours a day, that friction adds up. One instruction removes it for good, so every reply starts with the answer.
 
2. Show options before acting
Claude picks one approach and runs with it. You ask it to rewrite a paragraph and it changes the tone of the whole piece. You ask it to restructure a doc and it reorganizes things in a way that doesn't match how you think. Now you're fixing something you never asked it to touch. This instruction makes it lay out a few directions first, so what follows is what you wanted.
 
3. Be honest when you don't know
Claude will hand you a confident, detailed, fully wrong answer before it admits it isn't sure. It fills gaps with plausible dates, stats, quotes and facts that feel true but aren't, and the problem shows up later when it matters most. This makes it flag uncertainty up front instead of burying it.
 
4. Match length to the task
Ask a simple question and Claude writes 4 paragraphs. Ask for something complex and it gives you a skeleton that looks done but isn't. Neither helps. Length should match what the task needs, short when the answer is short, deep when the work demands it.
 
 
How Claude Behaves
5. Ask before big changes
You ask Claude to fix one paragraph and it rewrites the whole document. You ask it to shorten something and it cuts sections you needed. Every time, you lose something and rebuild it from memory. This turns every significant change into a checkpoint: Claude stops, tells you what it's about to do, and waits for your yes.
 
6. Stay on what was asked
Ask Claude to fix one thing and it will "improve" 5 others while it's in there, adjusting your phrasing, reorganizing your structure, rewriting sentences you were happy with. Now you're hunting through everything to find what changed. This keeps it on task: fix what was asked, leave the rest alone.
 
7. Always tell me what changed
Claude finishes and you're left scanning the output trying to work out what's different. Which sections changed? Did it cut anything? Did it add something? Without a summary you're doing a manual diff every time. This makes it close every task with a short recap.
 
8. Never act on my behalf without asking
As AI tools connect to your email, calendar, social accounts and documents, the risk of Claude doing something you didn't fully mean grows with every integration. Sending a message, posting content, sharing a doc, scheduling something, these have real consequences and they happen fast. This builds a hard wall around them.
 
 
Your Context
9. Tell Claude who you are
Claude doesn't know if you're an expert or a beginner, a founder or a freelancer, someone who wants technical depth or plain language. Without that, it guesses, and it's wrong as often as it's right. One paragraph about who you are calibrates every response from that point on.
 
10. Give Claude the context of your work
Every session, Claude starts with no idea what you're working on, who it's for, or what matters. So it gives you generic output, because it has nothing else to go on. A short context paragraph changes that. Suggestions start fitting your real situation.
 
11. Lock in your voice
Claude has a default writing style. It's fine, it's just not yours. It leans on certain phrases and a tone that doesn't match how you talk, so you end up editing everything back toward your own voice. Define your voice once and Claude writes in it from the first draft.
 
 
Memory and Continuity
12. Give Claude a memory file
Claude forgets everything between sessions. But it can write files, and files persist. This tells it to keep a MEMORY.md with every important decision you make together, what was decided, why, and what you ruled out. It reads that file at the start of each session, so it stops re-suggesting things you already tried.
 
13. End-of-session summary
You close the session, come back 2 days later, and burn 15 minutes reading old messages trying to remember where you were. This makes Claude write a session summary to MEMORY.md before you wrap up, so the next session opens with full context.
 
14. Log what didn't work
You try an approach and it takes 4 attempts to get something usable. Weeks later you're back with a similar task and Claude starts over with the same bad suggestions. An error log breaks that loop. Every failed approach gets written down, so next time Claude checks the log and skips to what worked.
 
15. List the facts that never change
Every project has permanent facts: constraints from past decisions, rules that exist for a reason, things that are always true about your work. Without this, Claude suggests things that contradict them or makes you explain the same context again. This gives it a foundation that applies to every session.
 
 
For Developers
The rules below are for people using Claude Code to write, review or manage code. If that's not you, everything above is enough. If it is, these 6 rules are what keep Claude from turning into a loose cannon in your codebase.
16. Stay in scope
Ask Claude to fix one bug and it will refactor 3 files, rename your variables, reorganize your imports and "improve" code you've worked with for months, all without asking. Some of those changes break things. Scope control is the whole difference between a precise tool and a loose cannon.
 
17. Confirm before anything destructive
Claude Code runs in your terminal with access to your file system. It will delete files, overwrite functions and drop database tables because you told it to, even if you didn't fully realize what you were saying. One misread instruction and hours of work are gone with no undo.
 
18. Hard stops
Deploying to production. Running migrations on a live database. Sending API calls to external services. These aren't "be careful" moments, they are full stops that need you consciously in the room saying yes.
 
19. Lock your tech stack
Without a defined stack, Claude suggests whatever framework it thinks is most popular and whatever package manager it defaults to. Often that's not what you use or what your team knows. Define the stack once and it stops suggesting things you don't want.
 
20. Show exactly what changed
Claude finishes and you're left scanning to figure out what's different. Which files changed? Did it touch anything else? Did it leave something unfinished? This makes it end with a file-level summary.
 
BONUS 21. The 4 rules behind the viral CLAUDE.md
Andrej Karpathy, former Director of AI at Tesla and a founding member of OpenAI, pointed out 4 behaviors that make Claude Code fail at coding. A developer turned them into 4 instructions in one CLAUDE.md file, and that file is the one that hit the top of GitHub Trending. Worth adding to any developer's setup.
 
 
Where to Start
CLAUDE.md is not just a developer tool. It's a permanent instruction file anyone who uses Claude seriously should set up before their first real session.
Rules 1 to 4 fix how Claude talks to you. Rules 5 to 8 stop it from changing things you didn't authorize. Rules 9 to 11 give it the context to produce output that fits your work. Rules 12 to 15 give it the closest thing to real memory that exists right now. Rules 16 to 21 are for developers who want Claude Code to behave like a precise tool.
Create the file. Paste in 3 instructions. Add more as you go.

article.plain_text:
An agent runs for four hours while you sleep. You wake up to one of two things: a finished feature that passes every test, or a pile of confident, wrong work that cost a couple hundred dollars to generate. Same model. Same prompt. The only thing that changed the outcome was the machinery wrapped around it.
Anthropic ran close to that experiment on purpose. The same model, on the same kind of task, inside two different harnesses. One setup burned around $200 a run. The other did comparable work for about $9. The model was identical. The harness was not. That 20x gap is the whole subject here, and almost nobody writing about loops will tell you where it comes from.
 
The shift is real. The skill everyone names is the wrong one.
You've seen the line by now. Stop prompting agents, start designing loops that prompt them. It's a real shift, and the people saying it have the direction right. A prompt gives you one answer and waits. A loop takes a goal and keeps working until it hits it, planning and doing and checking and going again on its own.
Here is where the advice stops being useful. Everyone teaches how to start the loop, and almost nobody teaches how to make it stop or reject its own bad work before it quietly drains the account. Starting a loop is easy. Claude Code ships /loop and /goal in the box. The hard half is the part that decides when the loop is allowed to be done and when it has to give up. That part is the actual job. The rest is plumbing.
 
The generator-evaluator contract
The single most important piece of a working loop is the split between the thing that does the work and the thing that judges it.
A model that grades its own work always passes. The loop needs a second one that can only say no.
This isn't a style preference. A model reviewing its own output is too generous a grader, because it wrote the thing and already believes it. Ask it "is this good" and it will talk itself into yes. So you separate the roles. One model, the generator, proposes. A second model, the evaluator, checks the work against a written contract and hands back a pass or a fail with reasons.
The word that carries the weight there is contract. Most people who set this up give the evaluator something soft: "score this 1 to 10, be honest." That fails, because the generator learns to produce output that scores well on a vague rubric instead of output that's correct. A real evaluator contract is specific and hard to game. It names the exact conditions for a pass. For code the contract is mechanical: the tests run green or they don't, the type checker sits at zero errors or it doesn't, and a red build doesn't negotiate. For research it's softer but still hard-edged, where every claim has to trace to a source and any number has to match the figure it cites. A section that contradicts an earlier one fails on the spot. The contract doesn't ask whether the work is good. It states exactly what a pass requires and what fails it.
Get this one thing right and you solve most of your quality problem before you touch anything else. Get it wrong and everything downstream is decoration.
 
Four controls that separate a loop from a demo
A demo loop runs once on stage and looks like magic. A production loop runs unattended for weeks and doesn't bankrupt you. Four controls make the difference.
Evaluator contract. The one that carries the most weight. Without a real gate that can fail the work, the loop just repeats itself and calls the repetition progress.
Stop condition. Every serious loop needs two ways to end: success, defined by the contract, and a hard ceiling that says after N tries, stop and report what still fails. A loop with no exit doesn't crash. It runs until it succeeds, breaks, or spends everything you have, and the silent version is the one that empties the account.
Cost ceiling. The control people skip until it hurts. Loops re-read their context on every pass, so the goal, the code so far, the last result, and the running list of failures all go back through the model each iteration, and the pile grows every time. Ten passes isn't ten prompts. It's ten prompts that each got bigger, and the moment you add a second model to evaluate, you've doubled the read. Set a token budget per run and a hard dollar cap, or the compounding sets one for you.
Escalation path. When the loop hits its ceiling without passing, it should hand the problem back with a clean summary of what it tried and where it stuck, instead of grinding on or shipping the half-finished version. A loop that knows when to tap you on the shoulder is worth more than one that pretends it finished.
 
Why a bad loop suddenly costs real money
None of this mattered much a year ago, because agents couldn't run long enough to do real damage. That changed. Claude Fable 5 posts 80.3% on SWE-bench Pro and holds a single task for hours instead of minutes. A longer run without a contract just means the mistake compounds further before anyone catches it.
Scale makes the point sharper. In one documented run, 16 agents working in parallel built a working C compiler at a compute cost around $20,000. The size isn't the risk; the missing gate is. With no evaluator to reject bad work, that's twenty thousand dollars of compute and nothing checking whether any of it was right. Which loops back to the number at the top: same model, same task, two harnesses, a cost of roughly $200 against about $9. The model never changed. The thing that could say no did.
 
Hermes Agent, and what "self-improving" actually means
"Self-improving" is the phrase doing the heavy lifting in most of this content, so it's worth being straight about it. Hermes Agent is a real, widely used open project, and its own benchmarks claim more than 175,000 stars and around 40% faster completion on repeated tasks. Treat the self-reported numbers with caution, but people run it every day.
Here is the honest read. Self-improving means the system gets more efficient at work it has already seen: it caches what worked and skips the dead ends it already hit, so a familiar job runs faster next time. That is real, and it is not the same as getting smarter on a problem it has never met. Quality on something new still comes from the harness and a contract that can reject a bad answer, not from the model quietly upgrading itself in the background.
 
The roadmap, from prompter to loop designer
You don't reach a production loop by starting with the loop. You reach it in an order most people skip, and skipping it is how loops blow up overnight. Four phases.
Phase one is harness setup. Before any automation, get one manual run reliable. Pick a task that genuinely repeats and that something can objectively check, then run it by hand in a normal Claude Code session, no /loop and no /goal yet. If you can't make it work once with you driving, automating it only fails faster.
Phase two wires the generator and the evaluator. Split the maker from the checker by putting the check in a separate subagent, so the model grading the work is not the model that wrote it. Write that subagent's contract as hard pass-or-fail conditions rather than a soft score, and feel free to give the checker a stronger model on higher effort. This phase decides most of your quality, so it earns the most of your time.
Phase three adds the four controls. Use hooks to enforce them at fixed points in the run, since a hook fires a real command while a line in a config file only suggests. Wrap the stop condition in /goal so the session ends on a defined condition instead of running forever, and set the token budget and the dollar cap right here, before anything runs unattended.
Phase four is run and observe. Schedule it with /loop on an interval, or push it to cron or GitHub Actions so it keeps running after you close the laptop. Watch the first several runs closely and track how often the evaluator rejects work and what it costs per accepted result. If it rejects almost nothing, your contract is too soft. If it rejects almost everything, the fault is in the generator or the task itself. You tune the contract, never the vibe.
 
The loop gets the credit. The stop condition saves the bill. The people whose agents are still running in week two aren't better at prompting than you are. They capped the spend before it ran away, and let a second model kill the work that wasn't good enough. That's the skill. Everything else is the model showing off.

article.plain_text:
When Fable 5 came out, I made the same mistake almost everyone else did.
I treated it like a more expensive version of Sonnet. Every new chat looked the same: rewrite this email, fix this bug, summarize this document or clean up this function. The answers were good, but not good enough to make me think, "Is this really what everyone's talking about?" After a few days, I honestly started wondering if people were overhyping it.
Then I gave it the kind of project I'd been avoiding for weeks because I knew it would be painful to do by hand.
Not another quick prompt, but something that actually mattered. A feature touching dozens of files, old code I didn't want to break and requirements that had already changed more than once. Fable wasn't just producing slightly better code. It kept making sensible trade-offs even as new requirements appeared and the implementation became more complicated. That's the kind of difference you simply don't notice in a five-minute task.
That's when everything clicked.
The biggest difference between Sonnet and Fable isn't how smart they are. It's the size of the problems they're designed to solve. Most people judge Fable after a few tiny prompts because that's the fastest way to compare models. The problem is that those are already tasks Sonnet handles extremely well, so the gap naturally looks small.
Fable starts making sense when every new decision depends on the decisions you've already made. Think about migrating an authentication system, replacing an old payment flow or untangling a codebase where changing one module forces changes across twenty others. That's where stronger reasoning finally becomes visible.
I stopped asking:
"Which model is smarter?"
Instead, I started asking:
"Does this project actually deserve Fable?"
From that point on, choosing between Sonnet and Fable stopped feeling like guesswork.
As a rule of thumb, I now use something incredibly simple:
Minutes by hand → Sonnet
Weeks by hand → Fable
It's obviously not a perfect rule, but it's been surprisingly accurate. Whenever I'm unsure which model to open, that's still the first question I ask myself.
The First Workflow I Completely Abandoned
The next thing I changed had nothing to do with prompts.
It was the order I used the models.
My workflow used to be simple. I'd open Fable, explain the idea and immediately ask it to start coding. Ten minutes later I'd remember another requirement, change the scope or realize I'd forgotten an important constraint. Before long, every conversation turned into a series of course corrections, and I started blaming the model for problems I'd created myself.
I was asking it to implement a project that was still changing. Every time I introduced a new requirement halfway through, the model had to rebuild its understanding of everything that came before. The more often I changed direction, the more inconsistent the implementation became.
Today, almost every serious project starts in Sonnet instead.
That's where I read the documentation, explore the existing codebase, compare different approaches and write a proper PRD. It's the phase where ideas are still changing, so I want a fast model that I don't mind interrupting every few minutes. By the time I switch to Fable, the project already has a clear objective, well-defined constraints and a plan that's unlikely to change halfway through implementation.
Instead of spending the first half of the conversation redefining the project, we're usually discussing architecture, trade-offs and implementation within the first few replies.
Today my workflow is almost always the same:
Sonnet
Research the problem
Read the documentation
Explore the existing code
Write the PRD
Fable
Review the implementation plan
Make architecture decisions
Build the difficult parts
Sonnet
Review the output
Write tests
Generate documentation
Clean everything up
I don't think this is the only correct way to use Claude.
It's simply the first workflow that kept producing consistent results, regardless of the project I threw at it.
The Planning Phase Changed Everything
For a while, I thought the quality of a project came down to writing better prompts.
It didn't.
The biggest improvement came from what happened before the first prompt was ever written. The projects that consistently went well all had one thing in common: I already understood what I was trying to build. There was a clear objective, a proper PRD, documented constraints and a realistic definition of what "done" actually meant. Fable wasn't spending its time filling in missing pieces because there were very few left to figure out.
Instead of asking Fable to write code immediately, I ask it to review the plan first. If the model misunderstands the goal, I'd rather find out before it touches a single file than after it has already modified thirty of them.
Reviewing a plan usually takes five minutes. Reworking an implementation that solved the wrong problem can easily take an hour or two. After a few projects, the better trade-off became obvious.
My planning prompt is intentionally simple:
 
I don't use this because it's a "magic prompt."
I use it because it forces both of us to agree on the problem before we start solving it. Most of the unnecessary rewrites disappeared once we started catching misunderstandings before a single file had been modified.
Context Beats Prompting Almost Every Time
One thing took me much longer to realize than it should have.
For weeks, I kept collecting prompts. Every few days someone would post "the ultimate Claude prompt" or "10 prompts every engineer should save," and I'd throw another one into my notes. I genuinely thought the next prompt would be the one that finally unlocked better results.
It almost never did.
The projects that consistently produced great results weren't the ones with the smartest prompts. They were the ones with the best context. A clear PRD, documentation that explained earlier decisions and enough background information that Fable didn't have to guess how the project worked. Once I noticed that pattern, I stopped spending time polishing prompts and started spending time improving the project itself.
The change showed up in an unexpected place.
The better my documentation became, the less I found myself explaining the same project over and over again. Instead of writing two hundred words trying to explain everything at once, I'd often get away with a single sentence because the model already understood what it was working on. The better the context became, the shorter my prompts became.
Today, before I start a large project, I make sure I can answer a few basic questions:
What exactly are we building?
What files are in scope?
What should never be changed?
What does "done" actually mean?
If those answers already exist in the PRD, the prompt itself becomes almost trivial. In my experience, improving those four answers has had a much bigger impact than rewriting prompts ever did.
The Projects That Finally Made Fable Click
It took me a while to notice a simple pattern.
Every time I caught myself thinking, "Fable doesn't feel that different," I was usually working on a small task. A quick refactor, a bug fix, a pull request review or a document summary. Those are all things Sonnet already handles extremely well, so expecting a dramatic difference was setting the wrong expectation from the start.
The projects that completely changed my opinion looked nothing like that.
They were the ones I'd been putting off because getting them wrong would create even more work later. Migrating an authentication system. Replacing an old payment flow. Refactoring a codebase where changing one module forces changes across dozens of others. That's where Fable consistently justified using the strongest model.
After a few months, I stopped deciding based on the task itself and started deciding based on the cost of making a mistake.
If getting something wrong only costs me five minutes, Sonnet is usually more than enough. If getting it wrong means restarting a migration, redesigning part of the architecture or untangling weeks of implementation, that's when I reach for Fable. Choosing between the two models stopped feeling subjective once I started looking at the cost of being wrong instead of the complexity of the prompt.
My routing rule today is still incredibly simple:
Use Sonnet for:
Research and documentation
Small bug fixes
Pull request reviews
Writing and editing
Exploring different approaches
Use Fable for:
Large migrations
Multi-file refactors
Architecture changes
Complex debugging
Long-running implementations
I don't treat this as a strict rulebook.
It's simply the pattern I've seen work consistently across enough projects that I no longer have to think about it every time I open Claude.
Final Thoughts
When Fable 5 launched, I thought the difference would show up in every prompt.
It doesn't.
The difference shows up when the project becomes large enough that good decisions matter more than fast answers. That's where Fable starts feeling less like a better chatbot and more like a senior engineer who can keep an entire project in their head without constantly losing context.
That's why I don't think of Fable as a model I should use more often.
I think of it as the model I should use more deliberately.
The biggest lesson wasn't that Fable is dramatically smarter than Sonnet. It was realizing that different models deserve different kinds of work. Once I stopped measuring them prompt by prompt and started judging them project by project, choosing between them became surprisingly straightforward.
Today, I don't open Fable because it's the strongest model.
I open it when the project is important enough that making the right decisions early will save me days of work later.
That's when it finally started living up to the hype.
